landing.subtitle: Your personal strength coach. Track progress, plan workouts, beat records — all offline, all free.
landing.feature1Desc: Build custom plans with days, exercises, sets, reps and rest times.
landing.feature2Desc: Charts, 1RM estimates, volume tracking and exercise history.
landing.feature3Desc: Auto weight suggestions based on your previous performance.
landing.feature4Desc: Share training plans with friends via QR code — instant import.
landing.feature5Desc: Link exercises together — auto-advance without rest between them.
landing.feature6Desc: Built-in timer for planks, isometrics and time-based exercises.
landing.feature7Desc: Rate your energy and sleep quality before each session.
landing.feature8Desc: GitHub-style streak grid — visualize your consistency over 6 months.
landing.feature9Desc: Unlock badges, get notified about new personal records.
landing.feature10Desc: Generate a beautiful workout summary image for social media.
landing.feature11Desc: Track chest, arms, waist, thighs and more over time.
landing.feature12Desc: Plan training phases: hypertrophy, strength, endurance, deload.
landing.ctaDesc: No account needed. Your data stays on your device.

plans.title: Training Plans
plans.deletePlanConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete this plan?
plans.muscleSummaryHint: Number of sets per muscle group per week
plans.noPhasesHint: Add periodization phases to structure your training cycle

workout.title: Workout
workout.discardConfirm: Are you sure you want to cancel the workout? Data will be lost.
workout.setActivePlan: Set an active plan in the Plans tab
workout.noWorkoutsYet: No workouts yet — start your first!
workout.incompleteWarning: Some exercises are incomplete or skipped
workout.skippedExercises: {{count}} exercise(s) will be marked as skipped
workout.confirmFinish: Great job! Ready to finish this workout?
workout.notesPlaceholder: How did you feel? Anything to note...
workout.deloadSuggestionDesc: You've been training hard for 4+ weeks. Reducing volume/intensity for a week can help recovery and future progress.

progress.title: Progress
progress.startTraining: Start training to see your progress
progress.noExerciseData: No exercise data for this day

settings.title: Settings
settings.qrTooLarge: Plan is too large for a QR code. Use file sharing instead.
settings.clearConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete ALL data? This cannot be undone!
settings.pushNotificationsDesc: Rest timer end notification (e.g. Apple Watch)

exerciseLibrary.title: Exercise Library
exerciseLibrary.deleteConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete this exercise?
exerciseLibrary.descriptionPlaceholder: Describe how to perform the exercise...

exerciseDesc.benchPress: Lie flat on a bench with feet firmly on the floor. Grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder-width, lower it to mid-chest with elbows at about 45°, then press up until arms are fully extended. Exhale on the way up. Keep your shoulder blades retracted and squeezed together throughout.
exerciseDesc.inclineBenchPress: Set the bench to 30–45°. Grip the barbell shoulder-width or slightly wider, lower it to the upper chest, and press up. Keep your back against the pad and avoid flaring elbows out too wide. Focuses on the upper chest and front deltoids.
exerciseDesc.declineBenchPress: Lie on a decline bench with legs secured. Lower the barbell to your lower chest, then press up. The decline angle shifts emphasis to the lower chest. Use a spotter or safety pins for heavier sets.
exerciseDesc.dumbbellBenchPress: Lie flat holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest level. Press up until arms are extended, then lower with control. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and even out strength imbalances. Keep wrists stacked over elbows.
exerciseDesc.dumbbellInclineBench: Set bench to 30–45° and press dumbbells from shoulder level upward. Lower slowly to a deep stretch at the bottom. Combines the ROM benefits of dumbbells with the upper-chest emphasis of the incline angle.
exerciseDesc.chestFly: Lie flat holding dumbbells above your chest with a slight bend in the elbows. Open arms wide in an arc until you feel a chest stretch, then squeeze them back together. Keep the elbow angle fixed—don't turn it into a press. Focus on the stretch and contraction.
exerciseDesc.cableCrossover: Stand between cable stations with handles set high or low. Step forward for stability and bring the handles together in front of you with slightly bent arms. Cables provide constant tension through the entire range. Squeeze the chest at the peak contraction.
exerciseDesc.dipsChest: Lean your torso forward about 30° on parallel bars. Lower until your shoulders are slightly below your elbows, then push up. The forward lean shifts the load onto the chest. Keep elbows slightly flared.
exerciseDesc.pushUps: Place hands slightly wider than shoulder-width on the floor. Lower your body as one unit until your chest nearly touches the ground, then press up. Keep your core tight and hips in line with your shoulders. Exhale as you push up.
exerciseDesc.chestPressMachine: Sit with your back flat against the pad and grip the handles at chest height. Press forward until arms are nearly straight, then return with control. The machine guides the path, making it safe for heavy loads. Great for chest isolation without stabilizer fatigue.
exerciseDesc.pecDeck: Sit with your back against the pad and forearms against the arm pads. Bring the pads together in front of your chest by contracting the pecs, then return slowly. Keep your shoulders down and avoid shrugging. Excellent isolation for the inner chest.
exerciseDesc.inclineChestFly: Set the bench to 30° and perform flyes with dumbbells. Open arms wide until a stretch is felt in the upper chest, then bring them back together. Keep elbows slightly bent at a fixed angle. Targets the upper chest with an isolation movement.
exerciseDesc.smithBenchPress: Lie on a flat bench under the Smith machine bar. Unrack, lower to mid-chest, and press up along the guided track. The fixed bar path reduces stabilizer demands. Position the bench so the bar path aligns with mid-chest.
exerciseDesc.landminePress: Hold the end of a barbell anchored in a landmine attachment at shoulder height. Press up and slightly forward until arm is extended. Can be done standing or kneeling, one or two arms. Great for shoulder-friendly pressing.
exerciseDesc.deadlift: Stand with feet hip-width, barbell over mid-foot. Hinge at the hips, grip the bar just outside your legs, brace your core, and drive through your heels to stand up. Keep the bar close to your body and your back neutral throughout. The most fundamental full-body strength exercise.
exerciseDesc.barbellRow: Hinge forward about 45° with a flat back, grip the barbell shoulder-width. Pull the bar to your lower ribcage, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top. Lower with control. Avoid using momentum—keep your torso stable.
exerciseDesc.dumbbellRow: Place one hand and knee on a bench, other foot on the floor. Row the dumbbell to your hip, keeping elbow close to your body. Squeeze your lat at the top, then lower slowly. Allows full range of motion and corrects imbalances.
exerciseDesc.pullUps: Hang from a bar with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar, then lower with control. Initiate the movement by depressing your shoulder blades. The premier back-width exercise.
exerciseDesc.chinUps: Hang from a bar with an underhand (supinated) grip at shoulder-width. Pull up until your chin is over the bar. The underhand grip increases biceps involvement compared to pull-ups. Keep your core engaged to avoid swinging.
exerciseDesc.latPulldown: Sit at the machine with thighs secured under the pads. Pull the bar to your upper chest with a wide overhand grip, squeezing your lats. Return slowly to a full stretch overhead. Lean back slightly but avoid excessive torso swing.
exerciseDesc.seatedRow: Sit upright with feet on the platform, slight knee bend. Pull the handle to your lower ribcage, retracting your shoulder blades. Extend arms fully for a stretch on the return. Keep your chest lifted and avoid rounding your back.
exerciseDesc.cableRow: Stand or sit facing the cable machine pole. Pull the handle toward your torso, squeezing the back muscles. Control the weight on the return for constant tension. Can be done with various handles for different grips.
exerciseDesc.tBarRow: Straddle the T-bar or landmine barbell, hinge forward with a flat back. Pull the weight to your chest using a close grip. Lower under control. Heavy compound rowing movement that builds serious back thickness.
exerciseDesc.facePull: Set a cable at face height with a rope attachment. Pull toward your face, separating the rope ends and externally rotating your shoulders. Pause at the peak contraction. Essential for rear delt development and shoulder health.
exerciseDesc.pendlayRow: Set up like a deadlift with the barbell on the floor. With a flat back parallel to the ground, explosively row the bar to your lower chest, then lower it back to the floor. Each rep starts from a dead stop. Builds explosive back power.
exerciseDesc.chestSupportedRow: Lie face-down on an incline bench and row dumbbells or a barbell upward. The chest support eliminates lower-back strain and prevents cheating. Focus on a strong squeeze at the top. Excellent for strict back isolation.
exerciseDesc.reverseGripLatPulldown: Perform a lat pulldown with an underhand (supinated) grip at shoulder-width. Pull the bar to your upper chest, emphasizing the lower lats. Control the eccentric slowly. The underhand grip increases bicep engagement.
exerciseDesc.pullover: Lie across a bench supporting your upper back, hold a dumbbell overhead with both hands. Lower it behind your head with slightly bent arms, then pull it back over your chest. Stretches both the lats and chest. Keep your hips low for a deeper stretch.
exerciseDesc.rackPull: Set safety pins in a rack at knee height. Deadlift the barbell from this elevated position to lockout. Allows heavier loads than a full deadlift. Focuses on the upper portion—back, traps, and lockout strength.
exerciseDesc.landmineRow: Stand over a landmine barbell, straddle it, and row with one or both arms. The angled bar path provides a unique line of pull. Keep your back flat and pull to your hip. Great for building back thickness with reduced lower-back stress.
exerciseDesc.overheadPress: Stand with feet shoulder-width, barbell at collarbone height in a front-rack position. Press straight up overhead until arms lock out, then lower under control. Brace your core and squeeze your glutes for stability. The foundational overhead strength exercise.
exerciseDesc.dumbbellShoulderPress: Sit or stand holding dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward. Press overhead until arms are fully extended, then lower to ear level. Dumbbells allow natural wrist rotation and a freer path. Keep your core braced throughout.
exerciseDesc.lateralRaise: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides. Raise arms out to the sides until parallel with the floor, leading with your elbows. Lower under control. Use a slight forward lean and avoid swinging the weights up. Isolates the lateral deltoid.
exerciseDesc.frontRaise: Stand holding dumbbells or a plate in front of your thighs. Raise one or both arms forward to shoulder height, then lower slowly. Keep a slight bend in the elbows. Targets the anterior deltoid—use moderate weight for strict form.
exerciseDesc.rearDeltFly: Bend forward at the hips or use a reverse fly machine. Raise the dumbbells out to the sides with slightly bent elbows until arms are parallel to the floor. Squeeze the rear delts at the top. Control the lowering phase.
exerciseDesc.uprightRow: Hold a barbell or dumbbells with a shoulder-width grip in front of your thighs. Pull straight up along your body to chin height, leading with elbows. Lower under control. Avoid going too narrow which can impinge the shoulder.
exerciseDesc.arnoldPress: Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms facing you. As you press up, rotate your palms to face forward at the top. Reverse on the way down. The rotation engages all three deltoid heads through a large range of motion.
exerciseDesc.cableLateralRaise: Stand sideways to a low cable pulley, grab the handle with the far hand. Raise your arm out to the side to shoulder height, then lower slowly. Cables provide constant tension—especially at the top where dumbbells lose resistance.
exerciseDesc.machineShoulderPress: Sit in the shoulder-press machine with your back flat against the pad. Press the handles overhead until arms are nearly locked, then lower. The fixed path makes it safe for heavy pressing. Keep your feet flat on the floor.
exerciseDesc.luRaise: Stand holding light dumbbells. Perform a lateral raise to 90°, then rotate into a front raise overhead in one fluid motion, and reverse. Combines three shoulder movements into one. Use light weight—this is harder than it looks.
exerciseDesc.barbellCurl: Stand holding a barbell with an underhand grip at shoulder-width. Curl the bar up to shoulder height keeping your elbows pinned at your sides, then lower slowly. Avoid swinging your back. The fundamental mass-building bicep exercise.
exerciseDesc.dumbbellCurl: Stand or sit holding dumbbells at your sides with palms facing forward. Curl up while supinating your wrist for a full contraction, then lower under control. You can alternate arms or curl simultaneously. Keep elbows stationary.
exerciseDesc.hammerCurl: Hold dumbbells with a neutral grip (palms facing each other). Curl up keeping this grip throughout, then lower slowly. Targets the brachialis and brachioradialis in addition to the biceps. Builds arm thickness.
exerciseDesc.preacherCurl: Sit at a preacher bench with your upper arms flat on the pad. Curl the barbell or dumbbells up, then lower slowly to near-full extension. The pad eliminates cheating and momentum. Don't hyperextend at the bottom.
exerciseDesc.concentrationCurl: Sit on a bench, lean forward, and brace your elbow against your inner thigh. Curl the dumbbell up with a slow, controlled motion and squeeze at the top. Pure isolation—no body english possible. Great for building the peak.
exerciseDesc.cableCurl: Stand facing a low cable with a straight or EZ bar attachment. Curl up to shoulder height, squeezing the biceps, then lower slowly. Cables keep constant tension through the entire range. Avoid leaning back.
exerciseDesc.ezBarCurl: Hold an EZ curl bar at the angled grips with an underhand hold. Curl up to shoulder height, keeping elbows pinned at your sides, then lower with control. The angled grip reduces wrist strain compared to a straight bar.
exerciseDesc.inclineDumbbellCurl: Sit on a bench set to 45–60°, arms hanging down holding dumbbells. Curl up without moving your elbows forward. The incline pre-stretches the biceps for a stronger contraction. Keep your back flat against the pad.
exerciseDesc.spiderCurl: Lie face-down on an incline bench so your arms hang straight down. Curl the dumbbells or EZ bar up, squeezing hard at the top. The position eliminates momentum and keeps constant tension on the biceps. Excellent isolation exercise.
exerciseDesc.machineCurl: Sit in the bicep curl machine with your elbows on the pad. Curl the handles up and squeeze, then lower with control. The machine stabilizes the movement and eliminates cheating. Good for controlled high-rep work.
exerciseDesc.tricepPushdown: Stand at a cable machine with a straight or V-bar attachment at head height. Push down until your arms are fully extended, keeping elbows pinned at your sides. Return slowly to about 90°. Don't let elbows drift forward.
exerciseDesc.overheadTricepExtension: Hold a dumbbell or cable overhead with both hands or one arm. Lower behind your head by bending the elbows, then extend back up. Keep your upper arms close to your ears. Stretches and strengthens the long head of the tricep.
exerciseDesc.skullCrushers: Lie on a bench holding an EZ bar or dumbbells with arms extended over your chest. Lower toward your forehead by bending only at the elbows, then extend back up. Keep upper arms vertical. Use a controlled tempo to protect your elbows.
exerciseDesc.closeGripBenchPress: Lie on a flat bench and grip the barbell at shoulder-width or slightly narrower. Lower to your chest keeping elbows close to your body, then press up. Shifts emphasis from chest to triceps. A heavy compound tricep builder.
exerciseDesc.dipsTricep: Grip parallel bars and keep your torso upright (don't lean forward). Lower until elbows reach 90°, then push up to lockout. The upright position targets the triceps over the chest. Add weight with a belt once bodyweight is easy.
exerciseDesc.kickbacks: Hinge forward, support one arm on a bench. Hold a dumbbell and extend your arm straight back, squeezing the tricep at full extension. Lower under control. Keep your upper arm parallel to the floor throughout.
exerciseDesc.ropePushdown: Attach a rope to a high cable. Push down and spread the rope ends apart at the bottom for a full contraction. Return to 90° slowly. The rope allows wrist rotation that enhances the squeeze at the bottom.
exerciseDesc.diamondPushUps: Place your hands close together under your chest forming a diamond shape with thumbs and index fingers. Lower your chest to your hands, then push up. Much more tricep-dominant than standard push-ups. Keep your core tight.
exerciseDesc.squat: Place the barbell on your upper traps, feet shoulder-width with toes slightly out. Brace your core, sit back and down until thighs are at least parallel, then drive up through your heels. Keep your chest up and knees tracking over toes. The king of all exercises.
exerciseDesc.frontSquat: Rest the barbell on your front deltoids in a clean-grip or cross-arm position. Squat down keeping your torso very upright, elbows high. Drive up through mid-foot. Requires good ankle and wrist mobility. Greater quad emphasis than back squats.
exerciseDesc.legPress: Sit in the machine with your back flat against the pad, feet shoulder-width on the platform. Lower the sled until your knees reach about 90°, then press up without locking your knees. Don't let your lower back round off the pad.
exerciseDesc.legExtension: Sit in the machine with the pad on your lower shins. Extend your legs until fully straight, squeezing your quads at the top, then lower slowly. Adjust the back pad so your knees align with the machine's pivot. Pure quad isolation.
exerciseDesc.lunges: Stand with a barbell on your back or dumbbells at your sides. Step forward into a lunge until both knees are at 90°, then push back to standing. Keep your torso upright and front knee over your ankle. Alternate legs or do walking lunges.
exerciseDesc.bulgarianSplitSquat: Stand in a split stance with your rear foot elevated on a bench. Lower until your front thigh is parallel to the floor, then drive up. Hold dumbbells for load. Excellent for single-leg strength, balance, and glute activation.
exerciseDesc.hackSquat: Position yourself in the hack squat machine with shoulders under the pads. Lower the sled until your thighs are parallel, then press up. Feet placement affects emphasis—higher targets glutes, lower targets quads. Keep your lower back against the pad.
exerciseDesc.gobletSquat: Hold a dumbbell or kettlebell at your chest with both hands. Squat down between your knees, keeping your torso upright. Drive up through your heels. An excellent teaching squat for beginners and a great warm-up movement.
exerciseDesc.smithSquat: Stand under the Smith machine bar resting on your upper traps. Unrack and squat down to parallel, then stand up. Position your feet slightly ahead of the bar for a comfortable path. The guided bar allows focus on legs without balance demands.
exerciseDesc.sumoDeadlift: Stand with a wide stance, toes pointed out, grip the bar between your legs. Push the floor away with your legs and pull the bar up to lockout. Favors lifters with longer limbs. Engages the quads, glutes, and inner thighs more than conventional.
exerciseDesc.stepUps: Stand facing a bench or box holding dumbbells. Step up with one leg, driving through the heel to stand on top, then step down. Keep your torso upright. Adjust box height—higher places more demand on the glutes.
exerciseDesc.boxSquat: Set up a box behind you at parallel depth. Squat back onto the box, pause briefly while maintaining core tension, then stand up explosively. The pause eliminates the stretch reflex and builds starting strength. Keep the same form as a regular squat.
exerciseDesc.romanianDeadlift: Stand holding a barbell at hip height. Push your hips back while lowering the bar along your legs, keeping a slight knee bend and flat back. Lower until you feel a strong hamstring stretch, then drive hips forward to return. The primary hamstring mass builder.
exerciseDesc.legCurl: Lie face-down or sit in the leg curl machine with the pad behind your ankles. Curl your heels toward your glutes, squeeze at the top, then lower slowly. Adjust the machine so your knees align with the pivot point. Pure hamstring isolation.
exerciseDesc.stiffLegDeadlift: Stand holding a barbell, keep your legs nearly straight (minimal knee bend). Hinge at the hips and lower the bar toward your feet until you feel an intense hamstring stretch. Return by squeezing glutes and hamstrings. Keep your back flat throughout.
exerciseDesc.goodMornings: Place a barbell on your upper back as for squats. Hinge forward at the hips with a slight knee bend until your torso is near parallel to the floor. Drive hips forward to return. Strengthens the entire posterior chain. Start light—this exercise demands good form.
exerciseDesc.nordicHamstringCurl: Kneel on a pad with your ankles secured. Slowly lower your torso forward toward the floor, resisting with your hamstrings. Push off the floor lightly if needed and curl yourself back up. One of the best exercises for hamstring strength and injury prevention.
exerciseDesc.hipThrust: Sit with your upper back against a bench, barbell across your hips. Drive through your heels to raise your hips until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees. Squeeze glutes hard at the top. The number-one glute isolation exercise.
exerciseDesc.gluteBridge: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor. Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes until your body is straight from shoulders to knees. Hold briefly at the top. A simpler hip thrust variation requiring no bench.
exerciseDesc.cableKickback: Stand facing a low cable, attach an ankle strap. Kick one leg straight back against the cable resistance, squeezing your glute at the top. Return slowly. Keep your core tight and avoid arching your lower back.
exerciseDesc.hipAbduction: Sit in the hip abduction machine with pads against your outer thighs. Push your legs apart against the resistance, squeezing the outer glutes. Return slowly. Keep your back flat against the seat. Targets the gluteus medius.
exerciseDesc.standingCalfRaise: Stand on a calf raise machine or the edge of a step with the balls of your feet. Rise up onto your toes as high as possible, hold briefly, then lower your heels below the platform for a full stretch. Targets the gastrocnemius. Use a full range of motion.
exerciseDesc.seatedCalfRaise: Sit in the seated calf raise machine with the pad on your lower thighs. Rise up onto your toes, squeeze at the top, then lower for a deep stretch. The bent-knee position targets the soleus muscle underneath the gastrocnemius.
exerciseDesc.legPressCalfRaise: Sit in the leg press machine with only the balls of your feet on the lower edge of the platform. Press through your toes extending your ankles, then lower for a full stretch. Allows heavier calf training with a safe setup.
exerciseDesc.crunch: Lie on your back with knees bent and hands behind your head. Curl your upper body up by contracting your abs—lift only your shoulder blades off the floor. Lower slowly. Avoid pulling on your neck; let your abs do the work.
exerciseDesc.plank: Support your body on your forearms and toes, maintaining a straight line from head to heels. Brace your core and hold. Don't let your hips sag or pike up. Time your holds and progress by adding duration. The foundational core isometric exercise.
exerciseDesc.hangingLegRaise: Hang from a pull-up bar with arms straight. Raise your legs until they're parallel to the floor (or higher for more difficulty), then lower with control. Avoid swinging—pause briefly before each rep. Targets the entire rectus abdominis and hip flexors.
exerciseDesc.cableCrunch: Kneel below a high cable with a rope attachment behind your head. Crunch down toward the floor by flexing your spine, not your hips. Squeeze your abs at the bottom, then return slowly. Allows progressive overload on the abs unlike bodyweight crunches.
exerciseDesc.abWheel: Kneel on the floor holding an ab wheel with both hands. Roll forward extending your body as far as you can while keeping your core tight, then pull back using your abs. Avoid letting your lower back sag. An advanced core exercise—start with short range.
exerciseDesc.russianTwist: Sit with knees bent, lean back slightly, and hold a weight at chest level. Rotate your torso to one side, then the other—that's one rep. Keep your chest up and abs braced. Targets the obliques. Perform slowly for maximum control.
exerciseDesc.declineCrunch: Lie on a decline bench with legs secured at the top. Perform a crunch by curling your upper body up, contracting the abs. Lower slowly. The decline increases the range of motion and resistance compared to flat crunches.
exerciseDesc.bicycleCrunch: Lie on your back with hands behind your head and legs raised. Bring one knee toward your chest while rotating your opposite elbow toward it. Alternate sides in a pedaling motion. Move slowly and deliberately—don't rush through the reps.
exerciseDesc.cableWoodchop: Set a cable high or low. Stand sideways to the machine, grip the handle, and rotate your torso diagonally across your body. Control the return. The rotational pattern builds functional core strength and targets the obliques.
exerciseDesc.dragonFlag: Lie on a bench gripping the edges behind your head. Raise your entire body into a straight line, then lower slowly keeping your body rigid. Only your upper back and shoulders should stay on the bench. An advanced exercise requiring extreme core strength.
exerciseDesc.pallofPress: Stand sideways to a cable machine at chest height. Hold the handle at your sternum, then press it straight out in front of you. Hold briefly, then return. Resist the cable's rotational pull—the core works by preventing movement. An excellent anti-rotation exercise.
exerciseDesc.shrugs: Stand holding a barbell in front of your thighs with arms straight. Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears, squeeze at the top, then lower. Don't roll your shoulders. Use heavy weight with a controlled tempo for trap development.
exerciseDesc.dumbbellShrugs: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Shrug straight up, squeezing your traps at the top, then lower slowly. Dumbbells allow a more natural arm position and slightly greater range of motion than a barbell.
exerciseDesc.wristCurl: Sit with forearms resting on your thighs, palms up, holding a barbell or dumbbells. Curl your wrists up, squeezing at the top, then lower slowly. Keep your forearms stationary. Builds forearm flexor strength and size.
exerciseDesc.reverseWristCurl: Sit with forearms on your thighs, palms down. Extend your wrists upward, then lower slowly. Use lighter weight than regular wrist curls—extensors are smaller muscles. Balances forearm development and prevents imbalances.
exerciseDesc.farmersWalk: Pick up heavy dumbbells or farmers handles and walk with them at your sides, maintaining an upright posture. Keep your shoulders back and core braced. Walk for distance or time. Builds grip strength, traps, core stability, and overall conditioning.

achievements.title: Achievements
achievements.everyDayTypeDesc: Train on every day of the week
